title: Andhra-Style Chana Masala for Rice
description: A thick, spicy, rice-friendly chana masala with onion-tomato base, curry leaves, tamarind, and fresh coriander. Designed to mix beautifully with hot rice.

subSteps: Measure 1 cup dried chana (kala chana or white chana).
Wash the soaked chana well under running water.
Add to a pressure cooker with 3 cups water, 1/4 tsp turmeric powder, 3/4 tsp salt, and 1 inch lightly crushed ginger.
Pressure cook for 6 whistles or until chana is cooked through and soft enough to press, but not mushy.
Let pressure release naturally.
Reserve the cooking water. Do not discard it.
VALIDATION: Chana should be soft when pressed between fingers but retain its shape. Cooking water should be clear to slightly cloudy.
Roughly chop 2 medium tomatoes (about 250g).
Slit 3 to 4 green chillies lengthwise.
Peel 6 garlic cloves.
Peel and measure 1 inch ginger piece.
Roughly chop 1/2 cup fresh coriander leaves with tender stems (lightly packed).
VALIDATION: All ingredients should be prepped and ready. Coriander leaves should be clean and fresh.

preparation.instruction: Crush part of the cooked chana.
subSteps: Take about 1/4 to 1/3 of the cooked chana from the pressure cooker.
Lightly crush it using a ladle or masher.
Keep the rest of the chana whole.
VALIDATION: Some chana should be crushed (not fully mashed), and most should remain whole.
Finely chop 2 medium onions (about 200g).
Soak a small marble-sized tamarind in 3 tbsp warm water and extract the pulp.
Measure out all spice powders: turmeric, coriander powder, red chilli powder.
VALIDATION: Onions should be uniformly and finely chopped. Tamarind should be well-soaked and pulpy.

ingredients.name: Dried Chana
ingredients.notes: kala chana or white chana, soaked overnight

ingredients.name: Turmeric Powder
ingredients.notes: 1/4 tsp for cooking chana, 1/4 tsp for masala

ingredients.name: Salt
ingredients.notes: for cooking chana, adjust to taste later

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.notes: 1 inch crushed for cooking chana, 1/2 inch for paste

ingredients.name: Fresh Coriander Leaves
ingredients.notes: lightly packed with tender stems, for paste

subSteps: Add tomatoes, green chillies, garlic, ginger, and 1/2 cup fresh coriander leaves to a mixer jar.
Add 2 to 3 tbsp water only if needed for grinding.
Grind to a smooth paste with no visible chunks.
Note: Do not add onion to this paste.
VALIDATION: Paste should be smooth, bright green-red colored, and aromatic. No raw ingredient pieces should be visible.
Heat 2.5 to 3 tbsp oil in a kadai or heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat.
Once hot, add 1/2 tsp mustard seeds and cover immediately.
Allow seeds to splutter for 15-20 seconds until crackling stops.
Add 1/2 tsp cumin seeds.
Then add curry leaves and a pinch of hing.
VALIDATION: Seeds should splutter vigorously. Strong aromatic smell should be present.
Add 2 finely chopped medium onions to the tempered oil.
Sauté on medium flame for 7-8 minutes, stirring often.
Cook until soft, golden, and lightly caramelized at the edges.
Do not rush this step.
VALIDATION: Onions should be soft and golden-brown. Lightly caramelized edges should be visible. Sweet aroma should be noticeable.

steps.instruction: Add and cook the masala paste.
subSteps: Add the prepared coriander-tomato paste to the pan.
Cook on medium flame, stirring often, until the raw smell disappears.
Continue cooking until the paste thickens.
Cook until the colour deepens and oil begins to separate lightly from the sides.
This usually takes 8 to 10 minutes depending on flame and moisture.
VALIDATION: Raw smell should disappear completely. Paste should be darker and glossy. Oil should separate slightly at the edges.
Add 1/4 tsp turmeric powder.
Add 1.5 tsp coriander powder.
Add 1 to 1.5 tsp red chilli powder (adjust to taste).
Mix well and fry for 1 to 2 minutes on low flame.
VALIDATION: Spices should be well incorporated. No raw powder smell should remain.
Add the tamarind extract (soaked in 3 tbsp warm water) to the masala.
Mix well.
Cook for 1 minute so the sourness blends into the masala instead of tasting sharp.
VALIDATION: Tamarind should be well distributed. Sourness should blend in gradually.
Add the whole cooked chana to the pan.
Add the lightly crushed chana.
Add about 3/4 to 1 cup reserved chana cooking water.
Add extra salt if needed.
Mix thoroughly so all chana is coated.
VALIDATION: All chana should be coated with masala. Consistency should be thicker than a normal curry but with some flow.
Cook on low to medium flame for 10 to 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
The masala should thicken and coat the chana.
The mixture should become slightly glossy.
Target consistency: thick flowing, not watery. Should be thicker than sambar but looser than a dry usli.
If too thick, add more reserved chana stock or hot water.
If too loose, simmer longer uncovered.
VALIDATION: Masala should coat the chana and have a thick, flowing consistency. When stirred, it should cling to the spoon.
Add 1/4 to 1/2 tsp garam masala.
Add 1/2 tsp jaggery (if using) for sweetness and to balance tamarind.
Add 2 tbsp chopped fresh coriander leaves.
Add 1 tsp ghee.
Mix well and simmer for 1 more minute.
Switch off the flame and rest for 5 minutes before serving.
VALIDATION: All finishing ingredients should be well incorporated. Aroma should be fragrant and balanced. Resting helps chana absorb the masala better.

adjustments.cooking_whistles: May need 1-2 extra whistles for softer texture

adjustments.ghee: 1 extra tsp for finishing instead of 1 tsp

seo.metaTitle: Andhra Chana Masala Recipe — Rice Curry | Authentic
seo.metaDescription: Thick, spicy Andhra-style chana masala for rice with curry leaves, tamarind, and fresh coriander. Perfect with hot rice. Easy recipe.
